bioParagraphs: Augustine was born in Roman North Africa in 354. Brilliant and restless, he pursued rhetoric, worldly ambition, and several competing philosophies before the prayers of his mother, St. Monica, and the preaching of St. Ambrose helped lead him to baptism in 387.

He later became Bishop of Hippo and one of the Church’s most influential theologians. His Confessions, The City of God, and writings on grace, memory, desire, and the human will continue to shape Western Christianity, earning him recognition as a Doctor of the Church.
medals.patronages: Converts to Christianity; theologians; printers; brewers.
medals.prayerTitle: “A Prayer Composed by St. Augustine”
medals.prayerText:
Before thy eyes, O Lord, we bring our sins, and with them compare the stripes we have received. If we weigh the evil we have done, we find what we suffer to be much less than what we deserve. "What we have committed far outweighs what we endure. We feel the punishment of sin, and yet we turn not from our willfulness in sinning. Our weakness faints under Thy scourges; but our perverseness is still the same. Our diseased mind is racked with pain, and our neck is as stiff as ever.

Our life is spent in sighs and grief; but in our actions we are not reformed. If Thou expect our amendment, we grow no better; if Thou take revenge, we are not able to subsist. When we are chastised, we acknowledge what we have done; but when thy visitation is over, we forget what we have wept for. If Thou stretch out Thy hand, we promise duty; if Thou suspend Thy sword, we keep not our promise. If Thou strike, we cry for pardon; and if Thou pardon, we provoke Thee again to strike.

Here, O Lord, are criminals confessing their guilt; we know that unless Thou forgive Thou mayst justly destroy us. Grant, without our merit, what we ask, O Almighty Father, Who out of nothing didst create us to ask Thee, through Christ our Lord. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Benedict was born in Nursia around 480 and withdrew from the corruption of Roman society to live as a hermit. Disciples soon gathered around him, and he eventually founded the monastery of Monte Cassino, which became a center of prayer, learning, and disciplined community life.

His Rule of St. Benedict joined prayer, work, study, obedience, hospitality, and moderation into a durable pattern of Christian life. Its influence across Europe was so great that Benedict is often called the father of Western monasticism and was named a patron of Europe.
medals.patronages: Europe; monks; religious communities; students; protection against poison and temptation.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer to St. Benedict
medals.prayerText:
O holy patriarch, Saint Benedict, who didst establish monastic life in the Church of the West, thou hast been blessed both in name and by God's grace. At the close of thy glorious and fruitful life, having received holy Viaticum, and, supported by the arms of thy disciples, thou didst stand with thine hands lifted toward heaven and didst happily give up thy angelic soul into the hands of thy creator.

O blessed Saint Benedict, thou hast promised to defend zealously from the snares of the enemy those who call upon thee during their last struggle with death; protect me on this and every day of my life with thy holy blessing, that I may never be separated from our Blessed Lord and the company of the blessed in heaven. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Very little can be established with certainty about Christopher beyond an ancient tradition that he was a martyr. The familiar medieval legend portrays him as a powerful ferryman who carried a child across a dangerous river, only to discover that the child was Christ bearing the weight of the world.

Christopher’s name means “Christ-bearer,” and his legend made him an enduring image of service, strength, and safe passage. His memorial was removed from the universal Roman calendar because of limited historical evidence, but he was not “unsainted” and remains widely venerated.
medals.patronages: Travelers; motorists; sailors; transportation workers; protection during journeys.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer in Honor of St. Christophorus
medals.prayerText: O God, Who didst make St. Christophorus a true Christ-bearer, who converted multitudes to the Christian faith, and who didst give him the grace to suffer for Thy sake the most cruel torments; through the intercession of this saint we implore Thee to protect us from sin, the only real evil. Preserve us, also, against harmful elementary forces, such as earthquake, lightning, fire, and flood. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Clare was born into a noble family in Assisi in 1194. Inspired by the preaching of St. Francis, she left wealth and social expectation behind, received a simple habit from Francis, and became the leader of the community later known as the Poor Clares.

She defended her community’s radical commitment to poverty and became the first woman known to write a religious rule approved by the pope. Tradition also remembers her confidence in the Eucharist when her convent was threatened, and her reported vision of a distant Mass led to her patronage of television.
medals.patronages: Television; telecommunications; eye disorders; embroiderers; the Poor Clares.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer of St. Clare during the attack on San Damiano
medals.prayerText: O Lord, do not give into the hands of the infidels the souls of those who acknowledge and praise Thee. Protect and preserve Thy handmaidens whom Thou hast redeemed with Thy precious blood.

medals.lifespan: fl. 9th century BC; earthly death not recorded in Scripture
bioParagraphs: Elijah was a prophet in the northern kingdom of Israel during the ninth century before Christ. He confronted King Ahab and Queen Jezebel, challenged the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel, defended the worship of the true God, and experienced God’s quiet presence at Horeb after fleeing in exhaustion.

Scripture says Elijah was taken up in a whirlwind with a chariot of fire. Catholic tradition venerates him as a saint, while the Carmelite Order honors him as its spiritual father because of his zeal, solitude, prayer, and association with Mount Carmel.
medals.patronages: The Carmelite Order; contemplatives; those seeking courage in defending the faith; prayers for rain in times of drought.
medals.prayerTitle: Elijah’s prayer on Mount Carmel, 3 Kings 18:36–37, Douay-Rheims
medals.prayerText: O Lord God of Abraham, and Isaac, and Israel, shew this day that thou art the God of Israel, and I thy servant, and that according to thy commandment I have done all these things. Hear me, O Lord, hear me: that this people may learn, that thou art the Lord God, and that thou hast turned their heart again.

bioParagraphs: Francis de Sales was born in Savoy in 1567 and became a priest during a period of intense religious conflict. When preaching met resistance, he distributed clear, patient explanations of Catholic belief in printed leaflets, combining intellectual firmness with unusual gentleness.

As Bishop of Geneva, governing from Annecy, he taught that holiness was possible in every state of life. His Introduction to the Devout Life and Treatise on the Love of God made practical spirituality accessible to laypeople, and he co-founded the Visitation Order with St. Jane Frances de Chantal.
medals.patronages: Writers; journalists; the Catholic press; deaf people; educators and authors.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer of St. Francis de Sales
medals.prayerText: O Blessed Virgin, dear mistress of mine, I salute thee, and revere thee with all my heart. Mother of Mercy, pray for me. Queen of Heaven, I commend my soul to thee. O my tender Mother, obtain that I may be loved by thy Son. Sweet hope with Jesus, sweet refuge of sinners, I throw myself at thy feet. Cause me to experience the effect of the power which thou dost possess with the Holy Trinity, O glorious Virgin Mary! Amen

bioParagraphs: George is honored as a soldier and martyr who died during the persecution of Christians under the Roman emperor Diocletian, probably in the early fourth century. Reliable details of his life are scarce, but devotion to him spread rapidly through both Eastern and Western Christianity.

The famous story of George slaying a dragon arose centuries later. Rather than a historical biography, it became a powerful image of Christian courage: the faithful soldier confronting evil, protecting the innocent, and refusing to worship false gods even under threat of death.
medals.patronages: Soldiers; cavalry; armorers; scouts; England; Georgia.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer in Honor of St. George
medals.prayerText: O God, who didst grant to St. George strength and constancy in the various torments which he sustained for our holy faith; we beseech Thee to preserve, through his intercession, our faith from wavering and doubt, so that we may serve Thee with a sincere heart faithfully unto death. Through Christ our Lord. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Homobonus was a twelfth-century cloth merchant from Cremona, Italy. A married layman rather than a priest or monk, he became known for honest business, generous almsgiving, care for the poor, devotion to the Cross, and efforts to reconcile neighbors divided by civic conflict.

He died in 1197 while attending Mass and was canonized only two years later after the people of Cremona petitioned for his recognition. His life presents commerce as a field for holiness when profit is governed by justice, charity, and responsibility toward the community.
medals.patronages: Businesspeople; entrepreneurs; merchants; tailors; shoemakers; clothworkers; Cremona.
medals.prayerTitle: Documented invocation
medals.prayerText: Saint Homobonus – — Pray for us!

bioParagraphs: Jerome was born around 347 in Dalmatia and received a classical education in Rome. After embracing an ascetic life, he studied Scripture and languages, served Pope Damasus, and eventually settled near Bethlehem, where he spent decades in scholarship, prayer, and spiritual direction.

He is best known for producing the Latin biblical translation that became the foundation of the Vulgate. Jerome’s fierce temperament sometimes appeared in his controversies, but his relentless devotion to the word of God made him one of the four great Latin Doctors of the Church.
medals.patronages: Translators; librarians; biblical scholars; students; archivists; archaeologists.
medals.prayerTitle: A Prayer of St. Jerome in Time of Agony
medals.prayerText:
Merciful Jesus, thou art my strength, my refuge, and my deliverer; in thee I have believed and hoped; in thee have I loved. Call me now, I beseech thee, and I will answer.--Stretch forth thy hand of mercy to the work of thy hands, and let me not perish, whom thou hast redeemed with thy precious blood.

It is now time for dust to return to dust, and my spirit to thee who gavest it. Open then, Lord, the gate of life, and receive me; receive me most merciful Lord, according to the multitude of thy tender mercies, who receivedst the thief on the cross, and now prepare my soul for hearing the same promise of mercy which he did. I am sick, O Lord, and my life is withering away; therefore I come to thee my physician.

Heal me then, my God, and I shall be healed: let me not be confounded; because I put my trust in thee. In thee have I hoped; let me not be cast off for ever.

But what am I, most merciful Lord, that I should speak thus boldly to thee?--I am a sinner, grown up in sin, a rotten carcass, a vessel of corruption, and food for worms. But spare me still, my God; for what victory is there in overthrowing me, who am but as dust before the wind? Forgive me all my sins, and deliver me from my distress.

Arise and help me, Lord; arise and let thy mercy plead for me. Let my prayer ascend before thee, and stretch forth thy hand to help me; for behold I am covered with sin, and have done evil in thy sight, and there is none can heal me but thou, my God. If thou hadst not paid my ransom, by dying on the cross, should not I have been for ever miserable? Remember then, O merciful Jesus, that I have a share in that price that was paid.

It was for me also, though most unworthy, thy blood was shed; let me, therefore, have a part in this mercy.

I confess I have many times offended against thee, and therefore am not worthy to be called thy child; but turn away thy face, I beseech thee, from my sins, and blot out all my iniquities. Deal not with me acccording to what I deserve, nor chastise me according to my iniquities; but help me, O God, my Saviour, and for the glory of thy name deliver me.

Now at this hour show mercy to me, and whenever I depart receive me into the number of thy family, that I may be one of those who are to praise thee for ever. Amen

bioParagraphs: Joan was born to a peasant family in France around 1412. As a teenager, she said that St. Michael, St. Catherine, and St. Margaret instructed her to aid the French cause during the Hundred Years’ War, leading to the relief of Orléans and the coronation of Charles VII.

Captured by Burgundian forces and handed to the English, Joan endured a politically driven ecclesiastical trial and was burned at the stake in 1431 at about nineteen years old. A later Church inquiry overturned the verdict in 1456, and she was canonized in 1920 as a model of courage, chastity, obedience, and fidelity under persecution.
medals.patronages: France; soldiers; prisoners and captives; those ridiculed for their faith; people facing unjust judgment.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayerful words from her trial testimony
medals.prayerText: If I am not in God’s grace, may He put me there; if I am, may He keep me there.

medals.lifespan: 1st century BC–1st century AD (traditional; exact dates unknown)
bioParagraphs: Joseph was the husband of Mary and the earthly guardian of Jesus. The Gospels describe him as a righteous man who responded to God’s direction with trust, taking Mary into his home, naming Jesus, protecting the Holy Family during the flight into Egypt, and settling with them in Nazareth.

Christian tradition honors Joseph for quiet obedience, faithful work, chastity, and steadfast care for those entrusted to him. Pope Pius IX declared him Patron of the Universal Church in 1870, and devotion to Joseph also emphasizes his protection of families, workers, and the dying.
medals.patronages: The Universal Church; fathers; families; workers; carpenters; immigrants; a happy death.
medals.prayerTitle: Invocation to St. Joseph
medals.prayerText: Saint Joseph, guardian of Jesus and spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary, pray for us.

bioParagraphs: Lawrence was one of the seven deacons serving the Church of Rome under Pope St. Sixtus II. When Roman authorities executed Sixtus during the persecution of Valerian in 258, Lawrence was given a few days to surrender the Church’s wealth.

Tradition says Lawrence distributed the remaining goods to the needy and then presented the poor, sick, widowed, and orphaned as the true treasures of the Church. He was martyred soon afterward; the familiar account of his death on a gridiron became one of Christianity’s most enduring martyr stories.
medals.patronages: Deacons; cooks and chefs; firefighters; the poor; comedians.
medals.prayerTitle: Collect from the Novena to St. Lawrence
medals.prayerText: Grant, we beseech Thee, Almighty God, the grace to quench the flames of our wicked desires, who didst give unto blessed Lawrence power to be more than conqueror in his fiery torments. Through Christ our Lord. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Lazarus lived in Bethany with his sisters, Martha and Mary, and the Gospel of John calls him a beloved friend of Jesus. After Lazarus had been dead four days, Jesus called him from the tomb, revealing His authority over death and foreshadowing the Resurrection.

Scripture says nothing certain about Lazarus’s later life, though Eastern tradition connects him with Cyprus and Western tradition with Marseille. In 2021, Pope Francis placed Saints Martha, Mary, and Lazarus together on the General Roman Calendar on July 29, emphasizing their household’s faith, hospitality, and friendship with Christ.
medals.patronages: The sick; the dying; the poor; gravediggers. Lepers are also frequently listed, though that patronage partly reflects an old conflation with the beggar Lazarus in Luke 16.
medals.prayerTitle: Documented invocation
medals.prayerText: Saint Lazarus of Bethany, pray for us!

bioParagraphs: The Gospels do not name the Roman soldier who pierced Christ’s side with a lance, nor do they explicitly identify him with the centurion who confessed Jesus as the Son of God. Later Christian tradition joined these figures and gave the soldier the name Longinus.

Medieval accounts describe Longinus converting, leaving military service, and eventually dying as a martyr. These later details cannot be established from Scripture, but his image became a striking symbol of conversion at the foot of the Cross and of faith born from encountering Christ’s sacrificial death.
medals.patronages: Soldiers; converts; people with blindness or eye ailments.
medals.prayerTitle: Documented invocation
medals.prayerText: Saint Longinus, pray for us!

bioParagraphs: Louis IX became King of France in 1226 while still a child and grew into a ruler renowned for personal prayer, legal reform, arbitration, charity, and direct service to the poor. He commissioned the Sainte-Chapelle in Paris to house relics of Christ’s Passion and sought to govern according to Christian moral law.

Louis led the Seventh and Eighth Crusades, was captured in Egypt during the first, and died near Tunis during the second in 1270. His crusading reflects the violent and complicated world of medieval Christendom, while his canonization in 1297 chiefly honored his justice, humility, penance, and care for his people.
medals.patronages: France; the French monarchy; Secular Franciscans; rulers and statesmen; barbers and hairdressers.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer from the Litany of St. Louis
medals.prayerText: O Lord, King of kings, Jesus Christ, Who didst love Saint Louis and didst lead him into the heavenly Kingdom: grant that by his intercession and good works, we may participate in his glory for all eternity. Who livest and reignest world without end. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Luke was a companion of St. Paul, who referred to him as “the beloved physician.” Christian tradition identifies him as the author of the Gospel according to Luke and the Acts of the Apostles, a two-part account moving from the life of Christ to the missionary growth of the early Church.

Luke’s writings place special emphasis on mercy, prayer, the Holy Spirit, women, the poor, and Christ’s concern for outsiders. A later tradition also describes him as an icon painter of the Virgin Mary, which helped make him a patron of artists as well as physicians.
medals.patronages: Physicians; surgeons; artists and iconographers; painters; notaries.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer for the feast of St. Luke
medals.prayerText: May thy holy Evangelist St. Luke, we beseech Thee, O Lord, be an intercessor for us; who, for the Honour of thy Name, always bore in his Body the mortification of the Cross: Through Our Lord, Jesus, Thy Son, Who liveth and reighneth with Thee in the unity of the Holy Spirit, God, world without end. Amen

bioParagraphs: Michael appears in Scripture as a heavenly prince who defends God’s people. The Book of Daniel presents him as Israel’s protector, the Letter of Jude shows him contending with the devil, and Revelation depicts Michael and his angels defeating the dragon.

The Church honors Michael as an archangel rather than a human saint. His name means “Who is like God?”—a declaration against pride—and Catholic devotion invokes him for protection in spiritual conflict, especially through the prayer composed under Pope Leo XIII.
medals.patronages: The Church; soldiers; police officers; paramedics; protection against spiritual evil.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer to St. Michael the Archangel
medals.prayerText: Saint Michael the Archangel, defend us in battle, be our protection against the malice and snares of the devil. May God rebuke him we humbly pray; and do thou, O Prince of the Heavenly host, by the power of God, thrust into hell Satan and all evil spirits who wander through the world for the ruin of souls. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Nicholas was a fourth-century bishop of Myra in Asia Minor, in modern-day Turkey. Although many stories about him developed long after his lifetime, the ancient and widespread devotion surrounding him consistently remembers a courageous pastor known for generosity, protection of the vulnerable, and defense of Christian faith.

The best-known tradition says that he secretly provided dowries for three impoverished young women, saving them from exploitation. Stories of his hidden gifts eventually contributed to the figure of Santa Claus, but the Christian Nicholas remains first of all a bishop whose charity preserved the dignity of people in danger.
medals.patronages: Children; sailors; travelers; merchants; pawnbrokers; the falsely accused.
medals.prayerTitle: Novena Prayer of Petition to St. Nicholas
medals.prayerText:
Glorious Nicholas, my own protector! from that bright throne where thou dost enjoy the vision of thy God, in pity turn thine eyes upon me; ask for me from God those graces and helps most seasonable in my present necessities, whether spiritual or temporal, and especially the grace of . . . . . . if such be expedient for my eternal welfare. Forget not, glorious and holy bishop, our true Sovereign Pontiff, the holy Church, and this pious city.

Bring back to the right way of salvation those who live steeped in sin, or buried in the darkness of ignorance, error, and heresy. Comfort the sorrowing, provide for the needy, strengthen the weak-hearted, defend the oppressed, help the sick; let all know the effects of thy powerful patronage with Him Who is the supreme giver of all good. Amen

bioParagraphs: Olaf Haraldsson was born around 995 and spent part of his youth as a Viking warrior. After receiving baptism, he returned to Norway, became king, and worked to strengthen both royal government and the country’s still-developing Christian institutions.

Olaf’s methods were shaped by the harsh politics of his age and were not always peaceful. Driven into exile, he returned and died at the Battle of Stiklestad in 1030; devotion spread quickly after his death, and he became the great national saint and “eternal king” of medieval Norway.
medals.patronages: Norway; Norwegian Christians; kings and Christian rulers.
medals.prayerTitle: Documented invocation
medals.prayerText: Saint Olaf, pray for us!

bioParagraphs: Paul Miki was born in Japan around 1562 and entered the Society of Jesus after studying in Jesuit schools. Known as a gifted preacher, he was preparing for priestly ordination when the ruler Toyotomi Hideyoshi ordered the arrest of Christians during a growing persecution.

Paul and twenty-five companions were forced on a winter march to Nagasaki and crucified there in 1597. From the cross, Paul proclaimed his Japanese identity, professed Christ, forgave those responsible for his death, and continued preaching until he was killed with a lance.
medals.patronages: Japan; Japanese Catholics; Christian martyrs; those persecuted for the faith.
medals.prayerTitle: Documented invocation
medals.prayerText: Saint Paul Miki and Companions, Martyrs, pray for us!

bioParagraphs: Peter was a fisherman from Bethsaida named Simon until Jesus called him “Cephas,” or “Rock.” He became the leading voice among the Twelve, confessed Jesus as the Messiah, witnessed the Transfiguration, failed through fear during the Passion, and was restored by the risen Christ.

Acts presents Peter as a chief preacher and leader of the first Christian community. Catholic tradition regards him as the first pope and holds that he was martyred in Rome under Nero, traditionally by crucifixion upside down because he considered himself unworthy to die exactly as Christ had.
medals.patronages: Popes; fishermen; net makers; shipbuilders; Rome; those seeking perseverance after failure.
medals.prayerTitle: St. Peter’s words to the risen Christ, John 21:17
medals.prayerText: Lord, you know everything; you know that I love you.

bioParagraphs: Rita was born in Italy around 1381 and lived successively as a wife, mother, widow, and Augustinian nun. Her marriage was deeply troubled, her husband was murdered in a feud, and her sons died young after she struggled to prevent them from pursuing revenge.

After entering religious life, Rita became known for prayer, penance, charity, peacemaking, and intense meditation on Christ’s Passion. A wound on her forehead was understood as a sign of sharing spiritually in the crown of thorns, and generations of Catholics came to seek her help in situations that appeared hopeless.
medals.patronages: Impossible and lost causes; difficult marriages; abuse victims; widows; infertility; parenthood; the sick.
medals.prayerTitle: Prayer to Saint Rita
medals.prayerText: O God, who didst deign to communicate so great grace to Saint Rita that she imitated Thine example in the love of enemies and bore in her heart and on her countenance the sacred marks of Thy love and passion: grant, we beseech Thee, by her merits and intercession, that we may love our enemies and ever contemplate with deep contrition the sorrows of Thy passion: who livest and reignest world without end. Amen.

bioParagraphs: Sebastian was a Christian serving in the Roman military during the persecution associated with Emperor Diocletian. Tradition says he secretly strengthened imprisoned Christians until his faith was discovered and he was shot with arrows and left for dead.

After surviving the first execution attempt, Sebastian openly confronted the emperor and was killed. His endurance made him a patron of athletes and soldiers, while medieval Christians invoked him during epidemics because the arrows in his martyrdom became symbols of the sudden “arrows” of plague.
medals.patronages: Athletes; archers; soldiers; protection against plague and epidemics; a holy death.
medals.prayerTitle: Documented invocation
medals.prayerText: Saint Sebastian, Martyr, pray for us!

bioParagraphs: The Synoptic Gospels identify Simon as a man from Cyrene who was compelled by Roman soldiers to carry Jesus’s cross on the road to Calvary. Mark also names him as the father of Alexander and Rufus, suggesting that his family may later have been known within the early Christian community.

Scripture reveals nothing certain about Simon after the Passion, and many later stories are devotional rather than historical. Even so, he remains a powerful image of discipleship that begins unexpectedly: a passerby drawn into Christ’s suffering who physically bears a burden that Jesus can no longer carry alone.
medals.patronages: People carrying heavy burdens; caregivers; manual laborers; passersby who aid the suffering. These are devotional associations rather than a universally defined Roman patronage.
medals.prayerTitle: Fifth Station prayer by St. Alphonsus de Liguori
medals.prayerText:
My most sweet Jesus, I will not refuse the Cross, as the Cyrenian did; I accept it; I embrace it. I accept in particular the death Thou hast destined for me; with all the pains that may accompany it; I unite it to Thy death, I offer it to Thee. Thou hast died for love of me; I will die for love of Thee, and to please Thee. Help me by Thy grace. I love Thee, Jesus my love; I repent of having offended Thee. Never permit me to offend Thee again.

Grant that I may love Thee always; and then do with me what Thou wilt.
